expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "It's okay, y'know, you just **nodded off** again."
Monica: "What's going on with you?"
Phoebe: "I got no sleep last night!"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 자기도 모르게 잠깐 졸다가 잠들다
expressions.nuance: 의도치 않게 순간적으로 잠든 상황에 쓴다. "fall asleep"보다 훨씬 가볍고 순간적인 졸음을 표현하며, 공공장소나 부적절한 타이밍에 깜빡 잠든 상황에 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: I **nodded off** during the meeting and missed the entire presentation. → 회의 중에 깜빡 졸아서 발표를 통째로 놓쳤다.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: And here's little Ben nodding off...

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (entering) So I nodded off a little.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: No-o-o! (To Monica) No? (She nods no.) No-o-o! Look Joey, here's the thing, Monica and I have decided to live together, here. So, I'm gonna be moving out man.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "You know what, I-I'd better **pass on** the game. I think I'm just gonna go home and think about my ex-wife and her lesbian lover."
Joey: "The hell with hockey, let's all do that!"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 제안이나 기회를 정중히 거절하다, 패스하다
expressions.nuance: "reject"나 "decline"보다 훨씬 가볍고 자연스러운 구어체. 강한 거부감 없이 그냥 하지 않겠다는 뉘앙스로, 음식이나 활동을 거절할 때 모두 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: "Are you coming to the party tonight?" "I'll **pass on** this one — I need an early night." → "오늘 파티 올 거야?" "이번엔 패스할게, 일찍 자야 해서."

occ.speaker: Ronni
occ.text: Now, y'see, most people, when their pets pass on, they want 'em sorta laid out like they're sleeping. But occasionally you get your person who wants them in a pose. Like, chasing their tail, (Demonstrates) or, uh, jumping to catch a frisbee.

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: I'm just gonna pass on the concert, 'cause I'm just not in a very Hootie place right now.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: OK, we'll just leave, and when we pass her on the stairs, she won't know it's me 'cause we've never met.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "C'mon, Ross!"
Ross: "Alright, alright, maybe it'll **take my mind off** it. Do you promise to buy me a big thumb finger?"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: ~에서 마음을 돌리다, ~를 잊게 해주다
expressions.nuance: 걱정이나 슬픔 같은 부정적인 생각에서 주의를 분산시킬 때 쓴다. "take my mind off things/it/work" 형태로 굳어져 자주 등장하며, 위로나 제안 문장에 자연스럽게 쓰인다.
expressions.example: Let's go see a movie. It'll **take your mind off** the interview tomorrow. → 영화 보러 가자. 내일 면접 걱정 좀 잊게 해줄 거야.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Chandler, you have to start getting over her. All right, if you play, you get some fresh air, maybe it'll take your mind off Janice, and if you don't play, everyone will be mad at you 'cause the teams won't be even. Come on.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Yes I do! And we're gonna go out, we're gonna have a good time, and take your mind off of childbirth and c-sections and-and giant baby heads stretching out...

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "Ninety-five, ninety-six, ninety-seven. See, I told you! Less than a hundred steps from our place to here."
Chandler: "You got **waaaay too much** free time."
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 훨씬 너무 ~하다, 도가 지나치게 ~하다
expressions.nuance: "too"를 강조하는 구어체 표현으로 "much too"보다 훨씬 캐주얼하고 강한 어감을 낸다. 과장이나 핀잔을 줄 때 즐겨 쓰며, 발음할 때 "way"를 늘여 말할수록 강도가 세진다.
expressions.example: That sweater is **way too expensive** for something I'd only wear once. → 한 번 입을 옷치고는 저 스웨터 진짜 너무 비싸다.

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Okay. It's-it's about Alan. There's something that you should know. I mean, there's really no easy way to say this.. uh.. I've decided to break up with Alan.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Alright. Once I got on the subway, right, and it was at night, and I rode it all the way to Brooklyn... just for the hell of it.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Ok, ok, here we go. (he crouches down near her stomach) Ok, where am I talking to, here? I mean, uh, well, there is one way that seems to offer a certain acoustical advantage, but...

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "Funny, my birthday was seven months ago."
Chandler: "Well, **aren't we Mr. 'The glass is half empty.'**"
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 역시 ~한 분이시네 (비꼬듯 상대방에게 어떤 특성을 딱지 붙이는 표현)
expressions.nuance: 상대방의 부정적이거나 예상 가능한 태도를 빈정댈 때 쓰는 패턴. 2인칭 상황에서 "we"를 써서 거리를 두면서 놀리는 효과를 낸다. 빈 칸에 특정 성격 유형을 넣어 응용 가능하다.
expressions.example: "This project is probably going to fail anyway." "**Aren't we Mr. Doom and Gloom** today." → "이 프로젝트 어차피 실패할 것 같아." "오늘도 역시 비관론자 등장이시네."

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "So c'mon, you guys, **tell me all the dirt!**"
Kiki: "Well, the biggest news is still you dumping Barry at the altar!"
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 최신 가십, 뒷이야기, 비밀스러운 소식 전부
expressions.nuance: "dirt"는 구어체로 누군가에 대한 가십이나 비밀스러운 정보를 뜻한다. "spill the tea"와 유사하지만 더 오래된 표현. 친한 친구 사이에서 오래간만에 만났을 때 근황 토크를 요청할 때 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: You were at the reunion — come on, **give me all the dirt** on who showed up. → 너 동창회 갔었잖아 — 얼른 다 말해봐, 누가 왔는지.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Oh, yeah, last week you had a wonderful, nutty, chocolatey kind of a cakey pie thing. (Rachel gives him a dirty look) Nothing, just, just, I'm fine.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Well, ahem... you know, by the time we'd finished with all the dirty talk, it was kinda late... and we were both kind of exhausted, so uh...

occ.speaker: Phoebe
occ.text: Oh my God, it's all so elegant! When's the dirty stuff starting?

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "I'm so sorry, you guys. I didn't mean to **bring you down**."
Monica: "No, you were right. I don't have a plan."
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 상대방의 기분을 가라앉히다, 분위기를 망치다
expressions.nuance: 자신의 푸념이나 슬픈 이야기로 상대방 기분까지 망쳤을 때 사용하는 사과 표현. "I didn't mean to bring you down"은 즐거운 자리에서 무거운 이야기를 꺼낸 후에 쓰는 자주 쓰이는 패턴이다.
expressions.example: I didn't want to **bring everyone down**, so I kept the bad news to myself all evening. → 분위기 망치고 싶지 않아서 저녁 내내 나쁜 소식을 혼자만 알고 있었어.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Uh.. okay, sure! Thanks! (She hands him the broom and sits down.)

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: The ride broke down. So, Carol and I went behind a couple of those mechanical Dutch children... then they fixed the ride, and we were asked never to return to the Magic Kingdom.

occ.speaker: Nurse
occ.text: It's just her water breaking. Calm down, will you?

expressions.dialogue:
Phoebe: (waking suddenly) "Oh! What what what! ...Hi."
Ross: "It's okay, you just nodded off again."
Monica: "**What's going on with you?**"
Phoebe: "I got no sleep last night!"
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 요즘 무슨 일 있어? 뭔 일이야? (걱정하며 묻는 표현)
expressions.nuance: 단순한 안부 인사가 아니라 상대가 이상해 보이거나 힘들어 보일 때 걱정하며 쓰는 표현. "What's wrong?"보다 부드럽고 덜 직접적이며, 상대가 알아서 털어놓을 공간을 만들어 준다.
expressions.example: You've barely touched your food and you haven't said a word. **What's going on with you?** → 밥도 거의 안 먹고 아무 말도 안 하네. 무슨 일 있어?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: (trying desperately to change the subject) So, Ross, what's going on with you? Any stories? (Digs her elbow into his hand.) No news, no little anecdotes to share with the folks?

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Okay, what's going on with you?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Oh no! No! No-no-no-no! No, I mean come on that's-that's crazy-I mean that's crazy. So what's-what's going on with you? What is going on with you?

expressions.dialogue:
Rachel: "It was a plan. Y'know, it was clear. It was **figured out**, and now everything's just kinda like..."
Phoebe: "Floopy?"
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: (계획이나 상황을) 완전히 파악하다, 다 계획돼 있다
expressions.nuance: 미래에 대한 확실한 계획이나 답이 있다는 뜻. 주로 "I had it all figured out" 또는 "have everything figured out" 형태로 쓰이며, 과거형으로 쓰면 "그때는 다 알 것 같았지만 틀렸다"는 회상 뉘앙스가 된다.
expressions.example: At 22, I thought I had my whole life **figured out**. I was very wrong. → 스물둘 때는 내 인생이 다 계획된 줄 알았어. 완전히 틀렸지.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "I think he's shy."
Phoebe: "Yeah?"
Monica: "Yeah. I think you have to **draw him out**. And then- when you do- he's a preppy animal."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: (내성적인 사람을) 말하게 하다, 마음을 열게 만들다
expressions.nuance: 소극적이거나 조용한 사람을 격려해서 스스로 이야기하거나 자신을 표현하도록 유도하는 것. "get someone to open up"의 뉘앙스이며, 친해지는 과정이나 인터뷰 기술을 이야기할 때도 쓰인다.
expressions.example: She's very reserved at first, but once you **draw her out** she has the most fascinating stories. → 처음엔 되게 조용한데, 한번 마음을 열게 하면 정말 흥미로운 이야기가 쏟아져.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Wow! Uh okay, well uh... (He gets up, opens a drawer, and pulls out the ring.)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: But the drawer full of take-out menus is okay, right?

occ.speaker: Mr. Zelner
occ.text: It's kind of a risky joke Tag, and what is-what is this drawing I can't figure out what this is?

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: "Oh come on, admit it! **All things considered**, you had fun tonight."
Ross: "Fun? Where was the fun? Tell me specifically, which part was the fun part?"
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 모든 것을 감안하면, 따져보면 결국
expressions.nuance: 여러 부정적인 상황들이 있었음에도 불구하고 전체적으로 보면 나쁘지 않다는 평가를 내릴 때 쓰는 관용어. 주로 문장 앞에 오며, 역경 뒤 긍정적 마무리를 지을 때 어울린다.
expressions.example: The trip had a few hiccups, but **all things considered**, it was a great weekend. → 여행 중 작은 사고들이 있었지만, 다 따져보면 정말 좋은 주말이었어.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "Excuse me, uh, that's, that's my puck."
Kid: "I found it. **Finders keepers, losers weepers.**"
Ross: (looks at Chandler for help)
expressions.speaker: Kid
expressions.meaning: 찾은 사람이 임자야 (잃어버린 건 운이 없는 거고, 발견한 사람이 갖는다는 어린이식 논리)
expressions.nuance: 미국 어린이들이 쓰는 놀이터 표현. 어른이 쓰면 유머러스하거나 유치하게 들린다. 논리적 설명 없이 "내가 찾았으니까 내 거"를 주장할 때만 쓰이며, 진지한 맥락에는 맞지 않는다.
expressions.example: I dropped a coin and my little cousin snatched it up. "**Finders keepers!**" she said, running off. → 동전을 떨어뜨렸더니 어린 사촌이 집어갔어. "찾은 사람이 임자야!" 하고 도망가더라.

expressions.dialogue: Ross: "Oh yeah? Well, **I'm rubber, you're glue**, whatever—" (to Chandler) "—can't do it." (to the kid) "Listen, uh— gimme back my puck."
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 나는 고무고 너는 풀이야 (네가 뭘 말해도 나한테 튕겨서 너한테 돌아가) — 어린이식 반박
expressions.nuance: 미국 어린이들이 비난을 돌려보낼 때 쓰는 라임. 전체 문장은 "I'm rubber, you're glue, whatever you say bounces off me and sticks to you." 어른이 쓰면 유치하지만 의도적으로 재미있게 쓸 수 있다. Ross처럼 포기하는 게 현실이다.
expressions.example: When her brother teased her, she shot back: "**I'm rubber, you're glue** — everything you say bounces off me!" → 남동생이 놀리자 그녀가 받아쳤다: "나는 고무고 너는 풀이야 — 네 말은 다 나한테 튕기거든!"

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Did they mention that I'm rubber and you're glue?

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "Look, I don't wanna make any trouble, okay, but I'm in a lot of pain here, alright? My face is dented."
Receptionist: "Well, you'll have to **wait your turn**."
Joey: "Well, how long do you think it'll be?"
expressions.speaker: Receptionist
expressions.meaning: 순서를 기다려라, 차례를 지켜라
expressions.nuance: 새치기하거나 급하게 요구하는 사람에게 쓰는 표현. 약간 딱딱하거나 귀찮다는 뉘앙스가 섞일 수 있다. "take a number" (번호표를 뽑아라)와도 비슷한 상황에서 쓰인다.
expressions.example: "Can I go next? I'm in a hurry." "Sir, you'll have to **wait your turn** like everyone else." → "제가 다음에 해도 될까요? 급해서요." "손님, 다른 분들처럼 순서를 기다리셔야 합니다."

occ.speaker: Monica
occ.text: Wait a minute, who told you? [turns to Chandler who's looking sheepish] You are dead meat.

occ.speaker: Richard
occ.text: Woah, woah, no wait a minute now. C'mon it's your turn. Oh c'mon. Ya know, I don't need the actual number, just a ballpark.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "Today's the day Carol and I first... **consummated our physical relationship**." (Joey is puzzled.) "Sex."
Joey: "The hell with hockey, let's all do that!"
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: (두 사람이) 처음으로 성관계를 갖다 — 직접적인 표현을 피하기 위한 격식체 완곡어법
expressions.nuance: "consummate"는 원래 결혼을 완성한다는 뜻의 격식 표현. Ross는 일부러 어렵고 점잖은 말로 돌려 말하지만, Joey는 못 알아듣고 결국 Ross가 "Sex"로 정리한다. 이처럼 민감한 주제를 완곡하게 표현하려 할 때 쓴다.
expressions.example: The novel hints that the characters finally **consummated their relationship** during the storm scene. → 소설에서 폭풍 장면 중에 두 인물이 마침내 관계를 맺었음을 암시한다.

expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: "Uh, if I were omnipotent for a day, I'd.. make myself omnipotent forever."
Rachel: "See, **there's always one guy.** (Mocking) 'If I had a wish, I'd wish for three more wishes.'"
expressions.speaker: Rachel
expressions.meaning: 항상 그런 식으로 나오는 사람이 꼭 한 명 있다
expressions.nuance: 규칙이나 취지를 비틀어 최대한 이득을 챙기려는 사람을 비꼬는 표현. 어떤 그룹에서나 "항상 그 한 놈"이 있다는 보편적 관찰을 담고 있어 공감을 유발하는 효과가 있다.
expressions.example: We said no phones at the dinner table, and sure enough, **there's always one guy** secretly texting under the napkin. → 밥 먹을 때 핸드폰 하지 말자 했는데, 역시나 냅킨 밑에서 몰래 문자 보내는 사람이 꼭 한 명 있더라.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "...you've just gotta figure at some point it's all gonna **come together**, and it's just gonna be... un-floopy."
Phoebe: "Oh, like that's a word."
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: (흩어져 있던 것들이) 맞아 떨어지다, 잘 풀리다
expressions.nuance: 힘든 시기를 보내는 사람을 격려할 때 "It'll all come together"는 매우 자주 쓰이는 위로 표현. 언제 어떻게 될지는 모르지만 결국 잘 될 거라는 막연하지만 따뜻한 메시지를 전달한다.
expressions.example: Keep working at it — these things take time, but it'll all **come together** eventually. → 계속 해봐 — 시간이 걸리는 법이지만, 결국 다 맞아 떨어질 거야.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Come on, we were great together. And not just at the fun stuff, but like, talking too.

occ.speaker: Minister
occ.text: You know, nothing makes God happier than when two people, any two people, come together in love. Friends, family, we're gathered here today to join Carol and Susan in holy matrimony.

occ.speaker: Jason
occ.text: Yeah. I mean y'know, we haven't been going out that long. Come on, we haven't even slept together yet. Huh.

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "Do you have a plan?"
Phoebe: "**I don't even have a 'pl'.**"
expressions.speaker: Phoebe
expressions.meaning: 계획은커녕 그 시작도 없다 ('plan'의 'pl'조차 없다는 유머러스한 과장)
expressions.nuance: "I don't even have a plan"에서 한 발 더 나아간 코믹한 과장법. 단어를 반쪽 잘라서 얼마나 아무것도 없는지를 강조한다. 이 패턴은 응용 가능해서 "I don't even have a 'bu'" (budget도 없다) 식으로 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: "Do you have a strategy for this?" "A strategy? I don't even have a **'str'**." → "이거 전략 있어?" "전략은요, 전(략)도 없어요."

expressions.expression: What are you doing? / I have no idea.
expressions.dialogue:
Chandler: (pretending to punch Ross in the stomach) "Huh? Huh? Huh?"
Ross: "**What are you doing?**"
Chandler: (stops) "**I have no idea.**"
expressions.speaker: Ross & Chandler
expressions.meaning: 지금 뭐 하는 거야? / 나도 몰라 — 자신의 행동을 스스로도 설명 못 하는 당혹스러운 순간
expressions.nuance: "I have no idea"가 단순한 모름이 아니라 자신도 왜 그러는지 모르겠다는 당혹감과 자기 비하적 유머를 표현한다. 어색한 상황을 해소하는 자기 인식의 코미디 패턴으로, 일상에서 자주 쓸 수 있다.
expressions.example: She caught herself humming a song she hated. "**What am I even doing?**" she muttered. → 자신이 싫어하는 노래를 흥얼거리는 걸 깨달았다. "나 지금 뭐 하는 거야?" 하고 중얼거렸다.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: (squatting and reading the instructions) I'm supposed to attach a brackety thing to the side things, using a bunch of these little worm guys. I have no brackety thing, I see no whim guys whatsoever and- I cannot feel my legs.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: So what are you gonna do?

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Chandler, what are you doing?

expressions.dialogue:
Monica: "Hey, Joey, what would you do if you were omnipotent?"
Joey: "Probably kill myself!"
Monica: "**..Excuse me?**"
Joey: "Hey, if Little Joey's dead, then I got no reason to live!"
expressions.speaker: Monica
expressions.meaning: 뭐라고요? — 충격을 받거나 황당해서 되묻는 표현
expressions.nuance: 단순히 못 들었을 때는 "Sorry?" 또는 "Pardon?"을 쓰지만, "Excuse me?"는 주로 충격받거나 황당할 때 쓴다. 억양에 따라 "지금 뭐라고 했어?"(놀람)가 될 수도, "감히 그런 말을?"(분노)이 될 수도 있다.
expressions.example: He said I should redo the entire report by 8 a.m. "**Excuse me?**" → 보고서 전체를 오전 8시까지 다시 쓰라고 했다. "뭐라고요?"

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Ugh. (To another customer that's leaving.) Excuse me, could you give this to that guy over there? (Hands him the coffee pot.) Go ahead. (He does so.) Thank you. (To the gang.) Sorry.  Okay, Las Vegas.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: No suds? Excuse me, hold on a second. (to woman) That's my friend's machine.

occ.speaker: Chandler
occ.text: Excuse me? You don't think I could get a Brian? Because I could get a Brian. Believe you me. ...I'm really not.

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: "I'd better pass on the game. I think I'm just gonna go home and think about my ex-wife and her lesbian lover."
Joey: "**The hell with hockey**, let's all do that!"
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: ~는 됐어, ~따윈 모르겠다, ~필요 없어
expressions.nuance: 앞서 중요하다고 생각했던 것을 갑자기 내팽개칠 때 쓰는 강한 구어체 표현. "To hell with ___"와 동일하며 약간 거친 어감이 있다. 포기나 해방감을 유머러스하게 표현할 때 효과적이다.
expressions.example: **The hell with** the diet — it's my birthday and I'm having the whole cake. → 다이어트 따위 알 게 뭐야 — 내 생일인데 케이크 다 먹을 거야.

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: Well then that's it. And if George and Adelaide can't accept that, then the hell with them. Look, if my parents didn't want me to marry you, no way that would have stopped me. Look, this is your wedding. Do it.

occ.speaker: Rachel
occ.text: Yeah, who's gonna eat all our food, and tie up our phone lines, and - is that my bra? What the hell you doin' with my bra?

occ.speaker: Mr. Greene
occ.text: ...what the hell does she want with half a boat...

expressions.dialogue:
Pizza Guy: "This one goes across the street, I must have given him yours. Oh, **bonehead, bonehead!**"
Monica: "Wait, was this a small mediterranean guy with curiously intelligent good looks?"
expressions.speaker: Pizza Guy
expressions.meaning: 멍청이, 얼간이 (자신 또는 타인의 어리석은 실수를 비하할 때)
expressions.nuance: "idiot"이나 "dummy"와 비슷하지만 더 캐주얼하고 덜 심한 표현. 이 장면처럼 자신의 실수에 자책할 때 주로 쓰며, 욕이라기보다는 가벼운 핀잔에 가깝다.
expressions.example: I left my laptop on the train. "**Bonehead!**" I said, sprinting back toward the platform. → 기차에 노트북을 두고 내렸다. "이 멍청아!" 하며 플랫폼으로 다시 뛰었다.

expressions.dialogue: Chandler: "...we've been here for over an hour, and a lot of people less sick than my friend have gone in. I mean, that guy with the toe thing? **Who's he sleeping with?**"
expressions.speaker: Chandler
expressions.meaning: 도대체 어떤 빽이 있는 거야? (직역은 '누구랑 자는 거야?'이지만, 실제로는 특별 대우를 받는 이유를 비꼬는 표현)
expressions.nuance: 성적인 의미가 아니라 연줄이나 특혜로 새치기한 사람에 대한 날카로운 비아냥. 부당한 특혜를 받는 상황에서 쓰는 표현이며, 직장이나 사회적 맥락에서도 응용 가능하다.
expressions.example: He got the corner office after one week. Honestly, **who is he sleeping with?** → 일주일 만에 코너 오피스를 받았어. 진짜, 도대체 어디 빽이 있는 거야?

expressions.dialogue:
Ross: (sarcastic) "Oh, **by the way**, great service tonight."
All: "Oh! Yeah!"
Guys: "Hockey!" (They go to leave but are blocked by Rachel's friends.)
expressions.speaker: Ross
expressions.meaning: 그나저나, 아 그리고, 덧붙여서
expressions.nuance: 주된 대화 흐름과 살짝 벗어난 추가 언급을 할 때 쓰는 연결어. 대화 중간이나 끝에 자유롭게 붙인다. 여기서 Ross는 자신들이 얼마나 형편없는 손님이었는지를 비꼬는 맥락에서 사용했다.
expressions.example: We're set for Friday at 7. **By the way**, can you bring your portable speaker? → 금요일 7시에 만나기로 했어. 아 그리고, 포터블 스피커 가져올 수 있어?

occ.speaker: Ross
occ.text: What a beautiful story. Hey, I'm fine by the way.

occ.speaker: Lowell
occ.text: Speaking for my people, I'd have to say no. By the way, your friend Brian from Payroll, he is.

occ.speaker: Janice
occ.text: By the way, Chandler. I cut you out of all my pictures. So if you want, I have a bag with just your heads.

expressions.dialogue:
Joey: "Hey! Here's the birthday boy! Ross, **check it out**: hockey tickets, Rangers-Penguins, tonight at the Garden, and we're taking you."
Chandler: "Happy birthday, pal!"
expressions.speaker: Joey
expressions.meaning: 이것 좀 봐, 봐봐 (흥미롭거나 신나는 것을 보여줄 때 주의를 끄는 말)
expressions.nuance: 무언가 신기하거나 좋은 것을 공유할 때 쓰는 캐주얼 표현. "Look at this"보다 훨씬 구어적이고 에너지가 넘친다. 명령형으로 쓰이며 콜론(:) 뒤에 보여주고 싶은 것을 바로 연결하는 패턴이 자연스럽다.
expressions.example: **Check it out** — they finally opened that ramen place we've been waiting for. → 봐봐 — 우리 기다리던 라멘집 드디어 열었어.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Check it out, Giants-Cowboys. (He has a pocket TV)

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Check it out, check it out. Heckles' high school yearbook.

occ.speaker: Joey
occ.text: Hey, hey, check it out, guess what I got.
